distractor_plausibility.note: q2 distractor 'commit creates extra branch that must be deleted manually' (score 2) is weak but not absurd — it relies on a conceptual conflation that a beginner might hold. No distractor scores 1.

lo_coverage.lo: Explain what a Git repository is and describe what it contains.
Describe what a commit is and explain why each commit should carry a clear commit message.
Explain what a branch is and state why developers create separate branches instead of working directly on the main branch.
Describe what merging does and identify when you would merge a branch.
Trace the state of a simple project through the create-branch → commit → merge sequence.
Distinguish between a repository, a commit, and a branch in your own words.

objective_coverage.lo6_note: q6 stem ('A branch and a commit are the same thing — both save your work. Which correction is most accurate?') directly elicits the repository/commit/branch distinction. Correct answer defines branch as an independent line of development and commit as a saved snapshot at a specific moment, explicitly stating they serve different purposes. Sidecar objective_ref matches LO6 text. Fix confirmed.

beginner_fairness_and_scope.notes: All stems use plain language. All Git commands referenced (git init, git checkout, git merge, git branch, git commit) are introduced in corpus §5.1–5.4 and §6. q4 two-step merge workflow maps directly to corpus §5.4 and §6 Step 4. q5 commit-chain trace (A, B, C, D, E) maps directly to corpus §5.3 diagram. q6 student-statement correction format is accessible to freshers and tests no out-of-scope concept. GitHub push/pull explicitly deferred in corpus §6 note and is not tested. No forward-reference violations detected.
